does: This paper introduces HAMU, a hardness-aware multi-objective unlearning algorithm that reformulates machine unlearning as a constrained optimization problem. The core contribution is quantifying the inherent difficulty of unlearning through a hardness measure based on similarity between forget and retain data, then deriving an algorithm that guarantees specified improvements in forget quality while minimizing utility degradation on retained data. Unlike prior weighted-loss approaches that lack formal guarantees, HAMU updates model weights guided by this hardness metric to adaptively balance the competing objectives of removing influence from specific training samples while preserving model performance on the remaining data.
connects: This work extends prior unlearning research that typically treated forget quality and retain utility as a simple weighted trade-off without formal guarantees. It builds on foundational unlearning literature studying data deletion and influence removal, while incorporating constrained optimization frameworks commonly used in multi-objective machine learning. The hardness quantification through data similarity relates to work on dataset similarity metrics and the difficulty of reconciling conflicting objectives. It responds to limitations in existing unlearning algorithms by introducing theoretical grounding, connecting to recent progress on principled approaches to privacy-utility trade-offs and the observation that some unlearning problems are inherently harder than others depending on overlap between forget and retain distributions.
enables: This framework opens several research directions in unlearning and privacy-preserving learning. The hardness measure provides a diagnostic tool that predicts when unlearning will incur high utility costs, enabling practitioners to assess feasibility before attempting deletion. The constrained optimization perspective could extend to other unlearning scenarios like class-conditional or feature-conditional unlearning, and the approach may apply to other privacy problems requiring similar trade-offs. The theoretical guarantees on improvement extent could inspire work on efficient unlearning with certified bounds, while the hardness quantification might inform better dataset curation or model training strategies to improve future unlearning performance.
